name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱصْطَفَىٰٓ ءَادَمَ وَنُوحًا وَءَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَءَالَ عِمْرَٰنَ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٣٣)
```

```english
Indeed Allah chose Adam and Noah, and the progeny of Abraham and the progeny of Imran above all the nations; (33)
```

```arabic
ذُرِّيَّةًۢ بَعْضُهَا مِنۢ بَعْضٍ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٣٤)
```

```english
some of them are descendants of the others, and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (34)
```

## (Commentary)

This is the opening of the accounts concerning Jesus, son of Mary (عيسى بن مريم), and what pertains to them, including the mention of the truth regarding them and the argumentation against the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) — *those to whom the Divine Scriptures were previously revealed* — concerning his matter. Through these two verses, what follows is linked to the preceding verses that addressed the condition of the People of the Book.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ اللَّهَ اصْطَفَىٰ آدَمَ وَنُوحًا** (Indeed Allah chose Adam and Noah...) to the end of the verse. Selection (الاصطفاء), as previously explained in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **لَقَدِ اصْطَفَيْنَاهُ فِي الدُّنْيَا** (We chose him in the world) [al-Baqarah: 130], means taking the purest part of a thing and refining it from whatever sullies it. Thus, it is close in meaning to "choosing" (الاختيار). In the stations of guardianship (الولاية) — *the state of proximity and divine authority* — it corresponds to the station of submission (الإسلام), which is the servant’s proceeding in the course of pure surrender to the command of his Lord in whatever He deems pleasing for him.

However, this is distinct from being "chosen over all the worlds" (الاصطفاء على العالمين). Had the intended meaning of selection here been that general sense, it would have been more appropriate to say "from the worlds" (من العالمين), which would have implied the exclusivity of submission to them and altered the meaning of the discourse. Thus, selection over the worlds is a type of choosing and prioritizing them over others in a matter or matters in which no one else shares.

Evidence for what we have mentioned regarding the difference in selection is His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذْ قَالَتِ الْمَلَائِكَةُ يَا مَرْيَمُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ اصْطَفَاكِ وَطَهَّرَكِ وَاصْطَفَاكِ عَلَىٰ نِسَاءِ الْعَالَمِينَ** (And when the angels said, ‘O Mary, Allah has chosen you and purified you, and He has chosen you above the women of all the worlds’) [Al ‘Imran: 42]. Here, He distinguished between the two selections; thus, one selection is not the same as the other.

The Glorified has mentioned among these chosen ones Adam and Noah. As for Adam, he was chosen over all the worlds by being the first vicegerent (خليفة) — *a representative or deputy* — of this human species whom Allah placed on the earth. The Exalted said: **وَإِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ إِنِّي جَاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً** (When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth’) [al-Baqarah: 30]. He was also the first for whom the door of repentance (التوبة) was opened. The Exalted said: **ثُمَّ اجْتَبَاهُ رَبُّهُ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدَىٰ** (Then his Lord chose him, and turned to him [clemently], and guided him) [Taha: 122]. Furthermore, he was the first for whom the religion (الدين) was legislated. The Exalted said: **فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّي هُدًى فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا يَضِلُّ وَلَا يَشْقَىٰ** (Then if there comes to you any guidance from Me, whoever follows My guidance shall neither go astray nor be wretched) [Taha: 123]. These are matters in which no one else shares with him, and what a great merit (منقبة) this is for him (عليه السلام).

As for Noah, he is the first of the five "Arch-Prophets" (أولو العزم) — *the messengers of strong resolve who brought new divine laws* — who possessed a Book and a Law (الشريعة), as previously explained in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللَّهُ النَّبِيِّينَ** (Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets...) [al-Baqarah: 213]. He is the second father of this species, and Allah, the Exalted, bestowed peace upon him among all the worlds. The Exalted said: **وَجَعَلْنَا ذُرِّيَّتَهُ هُمُ الْبَاقِينَ وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ فِي الْآخِرِينَ سَلَامٌ عَلَىٰ نُوحٍ فِي الْعَالَمِينَ** (And made his offspring the survivors, and left for him [a good name] among posterity: ‘Peace be to Noah throughout the worlds!’) [as-Saffat: 77-79].

Then the Glorified mentioned the Progeny of Abraham (آل إبراهيم) and the Progeny of ‘Imran (آل عمران) among these chosen ones. The term "Progeny" (الآل) refers to the elite or specific associates of a person. Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "It is said that *al-Al* (الآل) is a metathesis of *al-Ahl* (الأهل) — *family or kin*. Its diminutive form is *Uhayl*, except that it is specifically used in annexation to prominent rational beings, not to indefinite nouns, nor to times or places. One says 'the *Al* of so-and-so,' but one does not say 'the *Al* of a man,' nor 'the *Al* of such a time' or 'such a place.' Nor does one say 'the *Al* of the tailor'; rather, it is annexed to the most noble and virtuous. One says 'the *Al* of Allah' and 'the *Al* of the Sultan.' In contrast, *Ahl* is annexed to everything; one says 'the *Ahl* of Allah,' 'the *Ahl* of the tailor,' 'the *Ahl* of such a time,' and 'the *Ahl* of such a city.' It is also said that it is originally a name for the person himself, with the diminutive *Uwayl*, and it is used for those who have an essential specialty (اختصاص ذاتي) with a person, either through close kinship or through devotion (الموالاة)." This concludes the relevant portion of his words. Thus, what is meant by the Progeny of Abraham and the Progeny of ‘Imran are their elite from their families and those joined to them, as you have come to know.

As for the Progeny of Abraham, the apparent meaning of the term is the pure ones from his offspring, such as Isaac, Israel, and the prophets from the Children of Israel, as well as Ishmael and the pure ones from his offspring—their master being Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—and those joined to them in the stations of guardianship (الولاية). However, the mention of the Progeny of ‘Imran alongside the Progeny of Abraham indicates that the term was not used in that broad sense. For this ‘Imran is either the father of Mary or the father of Moses (عليه السلام). In either case, he is from the offspring of Abraham, as is his progeny. Since they have been mentioned separately from the Progeny of Abraham, the intended meaning of "Progeny of Abraham" here is some of his pure offspring, not all of them.

Allah, the Exalted, said in what He said: **أَمْ يَحْسُدُونَ النَّاسَ عَلَىٰ مَا آتَاهُمُ اللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِ فَقَدْ آتَيْنَا آلَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَآتَيْنَاهُم مُّلْكًا عَظِيمًا** (Do they envy the people for what Allah has given them out of His bounty? We have certainly given the Progeny of Abraham the Book and wisdom, and We have given them a great kingdom) [an-Nisa: 54]. This verse is in the context of rebuking the Children of Israel and censuring them, as becomes clear by referring to its context and the surrounding verses. From this, it appears that the intended meaning of "Progeny of Abraham" therein is other than the Children of Israel—meaning other than Isaac, Jacob, and the descendants of Jacob (who are the Children of Israel). Thus, nothing remains for the "Progeny of Abraham" except the pure ones from his offspring through the line of Ishmael, among whom are the Prophet and his Progeny (آله).

Furthermore, we shall explain, Allah willing, that the intended meaning of "the people" (الناس) in that verse is the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and that he is included in the Progeny of Abraham by the indication of the verse.

This is also suggested by His saying, the Exalted, at the conclusion of these verses: **إِنَّ أَوْلَى النَّاسِ بِإِبْرَاهِيمَ لَلَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُ وَهَٰذَا النَّبِيُّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** (Indeed the nearest of all people to Abraham are those who followed him, and this prophet and those who have faith...) [Al ‘Imran: 68], and His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذْ يَرْفَعُ إِبْرَاهِيمُ الْقَوَاعِدَ مِنَ الْبَيْتِ وَإِسْمَاعِيلُ رَبَّنَا تَقَبَّلْ مِنَّا إِنَّكَ أَنتَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ رَبَّنَا وَاجْعَلْنَا مُسْلِمَيْنِ لَكَ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِنَا أُمَّةً مُّسْلِمَةً لَّكَ وَأَرِنَا مَنَاسِكَنَا** — until He said — **رَبَّنَا وَابْعَثْ فِيهِمْ رَسُولًا مِّنْهُمْ يَتْلُو عَلَيْهِمْ آيَاتِكَ وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ** (As Abraham raised the foundations of the House with Ishmael, [they prayed]: ‘Our Lord, accept it from us! Indeed You are the All-hearing, the All-knowing. Our Lord, make us submissive to You, and [raise] from our progeny a nation submissive to You, and show us our holy rites... Our Lord, raise amongst them an apostle from among themselves, who should recite to them Your signs, and teach them the Book and wisdom, and purify them...’) [al-Baqarah: 127-129].

Therefore, the intended meaning of the Progeny of Abraham is the pure ones from his offspring through the line of Ishmael. The verse is not in the context of exclusivity (الحصر); thus, there is no contradiction between its not mentioning the selection of Abraham himself, or Moses, and the other pure prophets from his offspring through the line of Isaac, and what many other verses establish regarding their merits, the loftiness of their status, and the height of their station. These are very numerous verses that need not be cited here, for the affirmation of one thing does not necessitate the negation of others.

Similarly, this does not contradict what has been revealed concerning the Children of Israel in His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ وَرَزَقْنَاهُم مِّنَ الطَّيِّبَاتِ وَفَضَّلْنَاهُمْ عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ** (Certainly We gave the Children of Israel the Book, judgement and prophethood and We provided them with all the good things, and We preferred them over all the nations) [al-Jathiyah: 16]. All of this is evident.

Nor does their being preferred over the worlds contradict the preference of others over the worlds, nor the preference of others over them. For the preference of one people or various peoples over others only necessitates their precedence in a worldly or otherworldly virtue over those beneath them. If their preference over the people contradicted the preference of others, or if the preference of those mentioned in the verse—namely Adam, Noah, the Progeny of Abraham, and the Progeny of ‘Imran—over the worlds contradicted the preference of others over the worlds, it would necessitate a contradiction among those mentioned in the verse themselves; and this is obvious.

Furthermore, the preference of these over others does not contradict the occurrence of varying degrees of excellence (التفاضل) among themselves. Allah has preferred the prophets over all the worlds and preferred some of them over others. The Exalted said: **وَكُلًّا فَضَّلْنَا عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ** (...and each We graced over all the nations) [al-An‘am: 86], and He also said: **وَلَقَدْ فَضَّلْنَا بَعْضَ النَّبِيِّينَ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ** (...and We have certainly preferred some of the prophets over others) [al-Isra: 55].

As for the Progeny of ‘Imran, it appears that the intended ‘Imran is the father of Mary, as suggested by the fact that these two verses are followed by verses mentioning the story of the wife of ‘Imran and Mary, the daughter of ‘Imran. ‘Imran, the father of Mary, is repeatedly mentioned by name in the Noble Qur’an, whereas ‘Imran, the father of Moses, is not mentioned even in a single place where it is certain that he is the one intended. This supports the view that the ‘Imran intended in the verse is the father of Mary (عليها السلام). Accordingly, the Progeny of ‘Imran refers to Mary and Jesus (عليهما السلام), or the two of them and the wife of ‘Imran.

As for the claim that the Christians do not recognize the name of Mary’s father as ‘Imran, the Qur’an does not follow their whims.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ذُرِّيَّةً بَعْضُهَا مِن بَعْضٍ** (Offspring, one from the other). Offspring (الذرية) originally refers to young children, as mentioned by linguists, and was then used for children in general, which is the meaning intended in the verse. It is in the accusative case as an "explanatory apposition" (عطف بيان).

In His saying, "one from the other" (بعضها من بعض), there is an indication that every part assumed from them begins and ends from and to the other part. This necessitates that the whole is uniform in its parts (متشابه الأجزاء), where one part does not differ from another in its attributes and states. Since the discourse is about their selection, this implies that they are an offspring who do not differ in the attributes of virtue for which Allah chose them over the worlds; for there is no randomness (جزاف) or play (لعب) in Divine acts, among which is selection, which is the source of significant goods in the world.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ** (And Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing), meaning He is All-hearing of their speech which indicates their inner thoughts, and All-knowing of their inner thoughts and what is in their hearts. Thus, the sentence serves as a justification (تعليل) for their selection, just as His saying, "offspring, one from the other," serves as a justification for the inclusion of this group in the gift of selection. The sum of the discourse is that Allah chose these over the worlds, and the selection extended to all of them because they are an offspring whose individuals are similar—some returning to others in the submission of hearts and the steadfastness of speaking the truth. He graced them with selection over the worlds because He is All-hearing and All-knowing; He hears their words and knows what is in their hearts.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Uyun*, in the discourse of Imam al-Rida with al-Ma’mun: "Al-Ma’mun said: 'Has Allah preferred the Kinship (العترة) — *the Prophet's immediate family and descendants* — over the rest of the people?' Abu al-Hasan replied: 'Indeed, Allah has made clear the preference of the Kinship over the rest of the people in the decisive part of His Book.' Al-Ma’mun asked: 'Where is that in the Book of Allah?' Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) said to him: 'In His saying: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ اصْطَفَىٰ آدَمَ وَنُوحًا وَآلَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَآلَ عِمْرَانَ عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ ذُرِّيَّةً بَعْضُهَا مِن بَعْضٍ** (Indeed Allah chose Adam and Noah, and the Progeny of Abraham and the Progeny of ‘Imran above all the nations; offspring, one from the other)...'" the hadith.

In the *Tafsir* of al-‘Ayyashi, from Ahmad ibn Muhammad, from al-Rida, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon them both): "Whoever claims that the matter is finished has lied, for the Will (المشية) belongs to Allah regarding His creation; He intends what He wills and does what He desires. Allah said: **ذُرِّيَّةً بَعْضُهَا مِن بَعْضٍ وَاللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ** (Offspring, one from the other; and Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing). Its last is from its first, and its first is from its last. So if you are informed of something specific from it that it shall be, and it occurs in something else from it, then the report has indeed come to pass regarding what you were informed of."

**I say:** In this, there is an indication of what was mentioned in the preceding commentary regarding the meaning of His saying: **ذُرِّيَّةً بَعْضُهَا مِن بَعْضٍ** (Offspring, one from the other).

Also in it, from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): He recited this verse and said: "We are from them, and we are the remnant of that Kinship (العترة)."

**I say:** His saying (peace be upon him), "and we are the remnant of that Kinship," refers to the term *‘Itrah* (العترة), which according to its original meaning refers to the foundation upon which a thing relies. From this comes the use of *‘Itrah* for children and the nearest relatives of those who have passed. In other words, it is the "preserved pillar" within a clan. From this, it appears that he (peace be upon him) derived from the saying of the Exalted, "offspring, one from the other," that they are a preserved kinship extending from Adam to Noah to the Progeny of Abraham and the Progeny of ‘Imran. Herein lies the subtle point in mentioning Adam and Noah alongside the Progeny of Abraham and ‘Imran; it is an allusion to the continuity of the chain of selection (الاصطفاء).
